transcript.transcript: Hello, I'm Tom Brown, and this is Ty Jackson. I'm with the Emmet Orchards oral history project. Today, Ty and I are in Seattle, Washington. Today is Sunday, June 15th. Uh, we're in our home to talk about my experience with Emmet Orchards and family stories about them. This oral histories conducted with the support of the Idaho Historical Society as part of the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. Ty, thanks for being willing to ask me some questions today. Um, this interview, uh, helps me reflect and share my memories and experiences about the orchards. Uh, so before we jump into the questions, I give my permission, uh, to record this interview, and, uh, Um, I'm Tom Brown. t o m b r o w n. Tom, tell me about growing up in Idaho. What was your community like? Well, um, it was, um, it was a family, uh, experience. I, um, grew up with my parents, Maurice and Ruth Brown, my sisters, Sharon and Ruth. And, um, I was born in 1958. And so in the 60s, I remember the Kennedy assassination. Um, there were cousins together every, every, um, weekend at, uh, Grandma and Grandpa Brown's on the south slope in the orchards. And, um, I worked there. Um, I went to church with a lot of those cousins at the Emmett Bible Hall. Um, I went to school, uh, my, one of my best friends growing up was, uh, my first cousin, Alan Brown, and Alan, his sister Sherry. Our first cousin, Raymond Shaw. We were playing together every weekend at Grandma and Grandpa's orchards. Um, and so, um, I had a close knit group of friends in high school in touch with some of those to this day. And, um, in a nutshell, it was a it was a safe, uh, fun, um, hard working, faith based, um, community of all kinds of family that included lots of cousins. Um. Well, going on to the second question there, please share your early experience about Grandma and Grandpa Brown. Well, um, I can remember grandma making me breakfast, uh, on her wood burning stove, uh, in her basement kitchen in the old house that her uncle Charles Wills built in. Emmet and I would hear that kindling crackling, and I would smell the bacon, and she had put on the eggs to fry them. And she had a grandma, and grandpa had a calendar in their kitchen of all of the presidents. And she pointed out to me, this is President Lincoln. He freed the slaves. And I'm like, wow, um, I attribute my love of, of history to, to my Grandma Brown. Um, in their downstairs living area, they had tons of antiques. Uh, we called them old fashioned things. They included pictures of Emmett Orchards, uh, when Uncle Charlie had first planted them, um, pictures of family. Um, so grandma, um, would say things to me like. She grew up in Marsland, Nebraska. Uh, she would talk about her uncle Nyron Poole and her cousin Mae Brown, who I got to meet. She moved to Emmett because she was close to her first cousin, uh, Grandma Brown, Hazel Wills Brown, and Alan, uh. Alan Brown. So, um, it it was, um, it was she was very strong in my in my memory, uh, fun, talkative, caring. I specifically remember how being trained how to use toilet paper by Grandma Brown. Um, she made it, um, fun and, um, respectful, and I. I learned how to do it. Um. Grandpa Brown, uh, had was in most of my memory was, uh, hard of hearing. Um, so Conversations weren't, um, strong, but, uh, he would make me feel special. And other kids, too. Sitting around the breakfast table in their kitchen, uh, he would pour black coffee in my milk. And so we felt all grown up, uh, having having, uh, coffee in our milk at the kitchen table. Um. He had a dog named Sammy. Sammy didn't smell good. Um, and, uh, he had a red naugahyde chair that he would sit in, uh, and read the newspaper as he was waiting for for breakfast. Um, one of his expressions was, hurray for our side. Um, and, uh, um, I also have a vivid memory of him, um, uh, working in the orchards, driving the tractor, um, pulling up into the shed with the the trailer full of boxes of cherries and unloading those working hard on on hot summer days. Next question. Tell me about the cherry or cherry orchards. Oh, tell me about the cherry harvest. The cherry harvest. Oh my goodness. Um, uh, before dawn, uh, mom would wake me up and I have a memory of of laying in bed. My sisters were in the same room, and, uh, the cool air coming through the window at the end of June in the summer and it being pretty dark out sky outside, a hint of of light. And I would get up, drag out of bed with mom and my sisters and and we would go to the orchard. I probably slept on the way there. I was a small kid, like under five, and, um, the dew would be wet. Uh, we'd get our clothes wet. with, uh, grandpa or the Orchard boss would show us which tree to pick cherries on. I was short, so I got the low branches next to the ground. Um, mom, what time is it? Is it time for lunch? Is it time to go home? Um. I drank water out of the canteen. Uh, could barely fill up the bucket and and pour it into the box. Um, my sisters and mom would be moving ladders in and out of the tree, so, um, lunchtime was fun. Mom wrapped our sandwiches in waxed paper. Yeah. Um, then as I got older, um, my cousin Raymond and Alan and I, we were within two years of age of each other, Alan and I, just two weeks apart. We'd be assigned our own tree. And, um, Aunt Doris Shaw was orchard boss in the mid 60s when we were like, six, seven, eight, given our own tree and, um, I remember she would lecture us, um, be careful not to pick the stem. To pick the spur. The spur is next year's cherries crop. Just pick the the stem of the cherry and don't break off. Uh, the spur, that's your that's next year's crop. So, you know, we had we had spurs in our box of cherries, and we'd pick those out and try to be more careful. Okay. Yeah, yeah. Um, at the end of the day, thank God the end of the day would come. It would get hot. Uh, we would go to the, um, the front shed on the highway where our boxes would be weighed, and we would learn how many pounds we picked. I can't remember how much we were paid. Uh, cents for the pound Owned. I did earn enough money when I was seven years old to buy a Timex watch for about $7, and I was pretty excited by that. I earned enough to buy my own watch, um, and, uh, in short, uh, that's that's the cherry harvest. I'm sure I could tell more. Um, thanks for asking me these questions. I will, I, I just grabbed this off. Off the shelf. Um, that's my grandmother and grandfather, Brown, Hazel and Allen. Um, grandma is holding me. Grandpa's holding, um, his namesake, my first cousin, Allen Brown. Um, this was taken at Cox's dude ranch in Emmett in 1959. Uh, we were one year old babies. Um, thank you very much, Ty, for your questions. Um.

lines.line: # Introduction to the Oral History Interview

> The speaker, Tom Brown, introduces himself and Ty Jackson, who is conducting the interview. The interview is part of the Emmet Orchards oral history project, supported by the Idaho Historical Society. It aims to commemorate America 250 in Idaho by capturing Tom's memories and experiences related to Emmet Orchards. Tom gives his consent to record the interview and expresses his appreciation for the opportunity to reflect on his past.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Oral History Interview
metadata.synopsis: The speaker, Tom Brown, introduces himself and Ty Jackson, who is conducting the interview. The interview is part of the Emmet Orchards oral history project, supported by the Idaho Historical Society. It aims to commemorate America 250 in Idaho by capturing Tom's memories and experiences related to Emmet Orchards. Tom gives his consent to record the interview and expresses his appreciation for the opportunity to reflect on his past.

lines.line: > In concluding the interview, the speaker reflects on the memories shared during the session. He shows a photograph of himself as a baby with his grandparents, capturing a moment from 1959. This photo symbolizes the deep connections and cherished memories discussed throughout the interview. The speaker thanks Ty for the questions, indicating the importance of this oral history project in preserving personal and family histories.

metadata.title: Reflections and Conclusion
metadata.synopsis: In concluding the interview, the speaker reflects on the memories shared during the session. He shows a photograph of himself as a baby with his grandparents, capturing a moment from 1959. This photo symbolizes the deep connections and cherished memories discussed throughout the interview. The speaker thanks Ty for the questions, indicating the importance of this oral history project in preserving personal and family histories.

lines.line: > The speaker grew up in a close-knit, family-oriented community in Idaho during the 1960s. He recalls significant events like the Kennedy assassination and describes regular family gatherings at his grandparents' orchard. His childhood was filled with interactions with many cousins and friends, and he mentions attending church and school with them. The community was characterized by hard work, fun, and a strong faith-based foundation.

metadata.title: Childhood in Idaho
metadata.synopsis: The speaker grew up in a close-knit, family-oriented community in Idaho during the 1960s. He recalls significant events like the Kennedy assassination and describes regular family gatherings at his grandparents' orchard. His childhood was filled with interactions with many cousins and friends, and he mentions attending church and school with them. The community was characterized by hard work, fun, and a strong faith-based foundation.

lines.line: > The speaker shares fond memories of his grandparents, particularly his grandmother who made breakfast on a wood-burning stove and taught him about history. His grandmother's house was filled with antiques and family photos, which left a strong impression on him. He also recalls his grandfather, who was hard of hearing, making him feel special and teaching him small life lessons. These memories highlight the strong familial bonds and the influence his grandparents had on his upbringing.

metadata.title: Memories of Grandparents
metadata.synopsis: The speaker shares fond memories of his grandparents, particularly his grandmother who made breakfast on a wood-burning stove and taught him about history. His grandmother's house was filled with antiques and family photos, which left a strong impression on him. He also recalls his grandfather, who was hard of hearing, making him feel special and teaching him small life lessons. These memories highlight the strong familial bonds and the influence his grandparents had on his upbringing.

lines.line: > The speaker vividly describes the annual cherry harvests from his childhood. He recounts waking up before dawn, the physical labor involved in picking cherries, and the communal aspects like sharing lunches. He details the instructions given by the orchard boss to carefully pick cherries without damaging future crops. The speaker also mentions earning enough money from the harvest to buy a watch, illustrating the value of hard work from a young age.

metadata.title: Cherry Harvest Experiences

story.description: Tom Brown tells of early memories of his grandparents Hazel and Allen Brown and picking cherries as a boy in their orchard in Emmett
story.title: Tom Brown and Tai Jackson 2025 06 15
